attributes.description: Alerts when OS wait time delta exceeds 60 seconds within the window. Sustained waits point to storage/CPU pressure.
attributes.name: [SQL Server OTel] OS wait duration high

investigation_guide.blob:
## SQL Server OS wait duration high

### What fired
`INCREASE(sqlserver.os.wait.duration) > 60s` during the window.

### Why it matters
OS wait types (PAGEIOLATCH_SH, WRITELOG, SOS_SCHEDULER_YIELD) expose the dominant resource constraint. Sustained waits mean the DB is I/O- or CPU-bound.

### Triage
1. Split by wait_type to find the dominant category.
2. Correlate with disk latency / CPU metrics.

### Remediation
- Address the underlying resource pressure.

### Tuning
- 60s across all wait types in the window; dive deeper per wait_type.

esqlQuery.esql:
TS metrics-sqlserverreceiver.otel-default
// Cumulative wait time by wait type (counter); PAGEIOLATCH_SH, WRITELOG, SOS_SCHEDULER_YIELD indicate I/O/CPU pressure
| WHERE metrics.sqlserver.os.wait.duration IS NOT NULL
| STATS wait_increase = SUM(INCREASE(metrics.sqlserver.os.wait.duration))
  BY instance_id = COALESCE(resource.attributes.service.instance.id, host.name, "unknown"),
     wait_type = COALESCE(attributes.wait.type, wait.type, "")
// Alert when wait time increased by > 60s in the window (indicates sustained waits)
| WHERE wait_increase > 60.0
| KEEP instance_id, wait_type, wait_increase
